display_name: In symmetric IRB you route to remote hosts via the L3VNI, so missing remote /32s in the local table is expected
context: Verifying inter-subnet (L3) forwarding in an EVPN fabric and not finding remote host host-routes in the local routing table
observation: Remote host /32 (or /128) routes do not appear in a tenant VRF's routing table even though inter-subnet traffic works
naive_interpretation: Host routes are missing, so EVPN L3 advertisement is broken and inter-subnet routing must be failing
correct_interpretation: With symmetric IRB, a leaf does not need a /32 for every remote host. It routes the packet into the L3VNI toward the egress leaf's router-MAC, and the egress leaf does the final bridging. The local VRF only needs the remote subnet prefix (or even just the L3VNI path), not per-host routes. Absent remote /32s is the expected, scalable behavior of symmetric IRB — it is asymmetric IRB that would require the remote ARP/host state locally. Confirm the design before treating missing host routes as a fault.
why: RFC 9135 describes symmetric IRB where ingress and egress both route, bridging only into the local L3VNI, so per-host remote state is unnecessary
Symmetric IRB scales precisely because leaves do not import every remote host route — they forward on the L3VNI to the egress router-MAC
Asymmetric IRB, by contrast, requires the remote host's ARP/MAC locally; the two models produce different expected route-table contents
Working inter-subnet traffic with no local remote /32s is the signature of a correctly functioning symmetric IRB deployment

confirmation_commands.purpose: Confirm IP-prefix (Type-5) routes carry the L3VNI and router-MAC used for symmetric IRB forwarding
Verify the remote subnet prefix and its L3VNI next-hop exist even when per-host /32s do not
Identify the L3VNI bound to the tenant VRF to confirm symmetric IRB is in use

traps: Expecting per-host /32 routes for remote endpoints in a symmetric IRB design where they intentionally do not exist
Confusing symmetric and asymmetric IRB, which have genuinely different expected route-table contents
Declaring L3 EVPN broken when the Type-5 prefix and L3VNI path are present and traffic flows
Looking for the remote host's ARP entry locally in symmetric IRB, where the egress leaf owns that state

sources.title: RFC 9135 — Integrated Routing and Bridging in Ethernet VPN (EVPN)
